topic: A person finds their spouse's secret savings account
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope felt thick and illicit in my hand, the paper cheap and smelling faintly of stale coffee. Inside, nestled between utility bills, was a bank statement. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that burned in my chest. It was a statement from an account I had never seen, a sprawling collection of numbers that represented a life lived entirely outside the shared reality we had built. I traced a line with a trembling finger, the ink blurring under the pressure. When I finally looked up at Mark, his easy smile suddenly seemed brittle, and the sudden, cold knot tightening in my stomach made my hands shake so badly I had to grip the edge of the table. "What is this?" My voice came out thin, reedy, stripped bare of any warmth.

[story 2]

I found the hidden ledger tucked behind a loose floorboard in the spare room. The paper was yellowed, brittle, and the entries detailed months of meticulous, secretive transfers. A wave of nausea washed over me, so potent it made my vision swim. I stared at the figures—large sums accumulated over years, untraceable, hidden from me. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the wooden crate. I wanted to crumple the pages, to destroy the evidence of this profound betrayal, but my hands were locked. I could only stare at the meticulous planning, the sheer audacity of the deception, a profound sense of wrongness settling deep in my core.

[story 3]

It was an accidental discovery, a misplaced key leading to a locked drawer in the study. Inside, I found the documentation: a savings account set up years ago, completely separate from our joint finances. The reality hit me with the force of a physical blow. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I pushed the papers away as if they were contaminated, my gaze fixed on the mahogany desk, unable to meet anyone’s eyes. A strange, hollow emptiness expanded in my chest, a sensation of profound alienation from the person I thought I knew. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, needing distance from the tangible proof of this secret existence.
